questions.question: What was the name of the economic policy of 'opening up' implemented in the Soviet Union by Mikhail Gorbachev?
questions.explanation: Perestroika ('restructuring') was Mikhail Gorbachev's economic reform program introduced in 1986 to restructure the Soviet economy. Glasnost ('openness') was his parallel policy of increased transparency. Together they were intended to revitalize the USSR, but instead accelerated its collapse. The loosening of Soviet control over information and politics unleashed forces Gorbachev could not contain — leading to the dissolution of the USSR in 1991.
questions.memory_hook: Perestroika = restructuring (economic). Glasnost = openness (political). Gorbachev introduced both to save the Soviet Union. Instead, they ended it. He opened the doors to air out the room and the whole house blew away. The most consequential unintended consequence of the 20th century.

questions.question: The 'Shot Heard Round the World' that began the American Revolutionary War was fired at which Massachusetts town?
questions.explanation: The first shots of the American Revolutionary War were fired at Lexington and Concord, Massachusetts, on April 19, 1775 — referred to as 'The Shot Heard Round the World' from Ralph Waldo Emerson's 1837 poem. British troops were marching to seize colonial arms stores at Concord when they were met by colonial minutemen at Lexington. The subsequent battle at Concord's North Bridge began the revolution.
questions.memory_hook: First shots of American Revolution = Lexington and Concord, April 19, 1775. 'The Shot Heard Round the World' — a phrase from Emerson's poem, not a contemporary description. Paul Revere rode the night before to warn 'the British are coming.' The colonists were home defenders; the British were the foreign army.

questions.question: Which ancient Roman structure, completed in 80 AD, could hold between 50,000 and 80,000 spectators?
questions.explanation: The Colosseum (or Flavian Amphitheatre) in Rome was completed in 80 AD and could hold 50,000 to 80,000 spectators. It featured retractable awnings (velarium) for shade, numbered entrances for crowd control, and underground chambers (hypogeum) for gladiators and animals. Gladiatorial combat was abolished in 404 AD; wild animal hunts continued into the 6th century. The Colosseum is the most visited monument in the world.
questions.memory_hook: Colosseum = 80 AD, 80,000 capacity. Had retractable shade, numbered seats, and elevators — ancient Rome had better stadium infrastructure than many modern venues. The gladiators were professional athletes, not slaves as usually depicted. Most were free men or freed men who chose the career.

questions.question: The Scramble for Africa, in which European powers colonized the continent, primarily occurred during which century?
questions.explanation: The Scramble for Africa (roughly 1880-1914) saw European powers rapidly colonize almost all of Africa. The Berlin Conference of 1884-85 allowed European nations to negotiate colonial claims. By 1914, only Ethiopia and Liberia remained independent. Colonial powers drew borders with no regard for existing ethnic, linguistic, or political boundaries — creating tensions that continue to affect African nations today.
questions.memory_hook: Scramble for Africa = 1880-1914. Berlin Conference 1884-85 where Europeans divided Africa on a map without any Africans present. By 1914 the only independent African nations were Ethiopia and Liberia. The borders drawn then are largely the borders today — and many of Africa's conflicts trace directly to them.

questions.question: In 1980, the U.S. men's ice hockey team defeated the heavily favored Soviet Union at the Lake Placid Winter Olympics in a result dubbed 'the Miracle on Ice.' What was the final score?
questions.explanation: The U.S. defeated the Soviet Union 4-3 on February 22, 1980. The Soviets had won every Olympic gold medal since 1964 and were considered invincible. The U.S. team, made up of amateur and college players, went on to beat Finland to win the gold medal. Sports Illustrated called it the greatest sports moment of the 20th century.
questions.memory_hook: Miracle on Ice: USA 4, USSR 3. February 22, 1980. Amateur college kids vs. the best.

questions.question: The Olympic flag features five interlocking rings of blue, yellow, black, green, and red on a white background. What do the five rings collectively represent?
options: The five continents of the world
questions.answer: The five continents of the world
questions.explanation: Pierre de Coubertin designed the Olympic rings in 1912. The five rings represent the five continents: Africa, the Americas, Asia, Europe, and Oceania. The six colors (including white) were chosen because at least one appears in the flag of every nation in the world. The rings' interlocking design symbolizes friendship among nations.
questions.memory_hook: 5 Olympic rings = 5 continents. 6 colors cover every nation's flag.

questions.question: What sport uses a 'birdie,' a shuttlecock, and a racket?
questions.explanation: Badminton uses a shuttlecock (also called a birdie or shuttle), a feathered projectile hit over a net with lightweight rackets. It is one of the fastest racket sports — a shuttlecock can reach 493 km/h (306 mph) in elite men's doubles, making it the fastest object in any racket sport. Badminton became an Olympic sport in 1992 and is dominated by Asian nations, particularly China, Indonesia, and South Korea.
questions.memory_hook: Badminton shuttlecock = world's fastest projectile in racket sports. 493 km/h in elite doubles. Faster than any tennis serve, squash shot, or golf ball off the tee. Yet it's considered by many a backyard game. Elite badminton bears no resemblance to what you play in the backyard.

questions.question: Tupac Shakur was shot and killed in 1996 at what age?
questions.explanation: Tupac Shakur was shot on September 7, 1996, in Las Vegas following the Mike Tyson vs. Suge Knight boxing match, and died six days later on September 13, 1996, at age 25. His murder has never been officially solved. He released five albums during his lifetime and has had more posthumous releases than any artist in history, continuing to chart for years after his death.
questions.memory_hook: Tupac died at 25 in Las Vegas, 1996. Never solved. He's released more music posthumously than he did alive — Suge Knight controlled his catalog. One of music's greatest what-ifs: what would Tupac have made if he'd had a full career? He was 25 with the talent of someone twice his age.

questions.question: The original Star Wars film (1977) features a memorable bar scene with alien musicians. What is this band called?
questions.explanation: The Mos Eisley Cantina scene in Star Wars (1977) features a jazz-like alien band playing 'Mad About Me' (commonly called 'Cantina Band' or 'Mad About Me'). The music was composed by John Williams. The musician characters are actually Figrin D'an and the Modal Nodes, but the music is universally known as the 'Cantina Band.' It is one of the most recognizable pieces of film music ever composed.
questions.memory_hook: Cantina Band = the alien jazz in the Star Wars bar. One of the most recognizable film music pieces ever. John Williams wrote it to feel genuinely alien — jazz-influenced but in a different time signature. It's been reproduced on every instrument imaginable and never gets old.

questions.question: Which British band's final public performance was a rooftop concert in London in January 1969?
questions.explanation: The Beatles performed their last public concert on the rooftop of their Apple Corps headquarters at 3 Savile Row, London, on January 30, 1969. The impromptu concert was filmed for the documentary Let It Be (1970). Police eventually stopped the performance due to noise complaints. The concert ended with John Lennon saying 'I'd like to say thank you on behalf of the group and ourselves, and I hope we passed the audition.'
questions.memory_hook: Beatles' last concert = rooftop of Apple Corps, London, January 30, 1969. Police came to stop it. Lennon closed: 'I hope we passed the audition.' They broke up 14 months later. The band that changed music ended not at a stadium but on a rooftop, unannounced, with a handful of observers.

questions.question: Which body of water separates the islands of Great Britain and Ireland?
questions.explanation: The Irish Sea lies between Great Britain to the east and Ireland to the west, connected to the Celtic Sea in the south and the North Channel in the north. It is approximately 220 km long and 240 km wide. The English Channel separates England from France. The North Sea is to the east of Great Britain.
questions.memory_hook: Irish Sea = between Britain and Ireland. The English Channel is between England and France. People often confuse them. Britain has the English Channel to its south, the North Sea to its east, and the Irish Sea to its west.

questions.question: What is the name of the microscopic structures within cells that carry out protein synthesis?
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are tiny molecular machines found in all living cells that read the instructions in messenger RNA (mRNA) and assemble amino acids into proteins. They can be free-floating in the cytoplasm or attached to the endoplasmic reticulum. Ribosomes are composed of ribosomal RNA and proteins. The antibiotic family (including streptomycin) works by blocking bacterial ribosomes, killing bacteria while leaving human cells unharmed.
questions.memory_hook: Ribosomes = protein factories. mRNA comes in, protein comes out. Antibiotics like streptomycin target bacterial ribosomes — bacteria have slightly different ribosomes than humans, so the drug kills them without harming us. Ribosomes are so fundamental that all life on Earth uses them.
